title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
subtitle: Redesigning the clinical scanning workflow to improve speed, clarity, and decision-making in real treatment conditions.

slides.title:
Clinical Scanning
Workflow
slides.description: Align Technology powers digital dentistry chair-side. Their scanning workflow — RX, Scan, and View — had friction at every phase. We redesigned all three into one continuous system.

slides.label: The System
slides.title: A system used during live treatment
slides.content: iTero is used while the patient is in the chair. The workflow moves through three connected phases — RX (setup), Scan (capture), and View (validation). Decisions in each phase directly affect the next.
issues: Breakdowns in any phase impact the full workflow

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: The system grew. The experience didn't.
slides.content: RX, Scan, and View were built up over time — each piece added without a shared logic connecting them. What should feel like one continuous workflow became three separate systems stitched together.
issues: Setup blocks users before they can start scanning
Procedure and tooth selection require too much mental effort
Scanning stops every time a tool needs adjusting
Tool feedback is unclear — users can't tell if it worked
Nothing signals when a scan is actually complete
Users lose confidence and fall out of flow

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What we heard in the room
slides.content: We observed clinicians during live scanning sessions and followed up with interviews. The same frustrations came up again and again — not as complaints about the software, but as accepted parts of the workflow.

quotes.text: The patient popup interrupts me before I even start.
quotes.author: Dr. Yael Levi

quotes.text: I change the procedure multiple times before I even begin scanning.
quotes.author: Dr. Amir Cohen

quotes.text: The moment I go looking for a tool, I lose my rhythm completely.
quotes.author: Dr. Noa Ben-David

quotes.text: I never fully trust the result the first time I see it.
quotes.author: Dr. Efrat Mizrahi

quotes.text: Even when I'm done, I keep going back to check.
quotes.author: Dr. Daniel Katz

quotes.text: I never know if I'm selecting the right tooth.
quotes.author: Dr. Lior Haddad

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: Four problems. Four things to get right.

goals.title: Let clinicians start without friction
goals.description: Remove the setup gates that block users before a scan can even begin.

goals.title: Keep the scanning motion unbroken
goals.description: Stop the interface from interrupting the physical act of scanning.

goals.title: Make every tool and state self-explanatory
goals.description: Clarity should be built in — not something users figure out over time.

goals.title: Make results feel trustworthy on first view
goals.description: Give users a clear signal that the scan is done, so they don't have to keep checking.

kpis: Time from RX entry to first scan action

slides.label: Interactive Prototype
slides.title: Testing decisions before they became real
slides.content: We built a fully interactive prototype using Claude Code to simulate the real scanning workflow — testing design decisions under realistic clinical conditions before anything went to development.
bullets2: Real workflow simulation, not just static screens
Friction points caught before development began
Changes tested the same day they were made

image.caption: Interactive prototype built with Claude Code

slides.title: RX
slides.subtitle: The prescription step where clinicians define the patient, procedure, and case details before scanning begins.

slides.label: RX Setup
slides.title: Getting started shouldn't feel like a form
slides.beforeDescription: A rigid setup sequence blocked users before scanning could even begin.
slides.afterDescription: Users start immediately and fill in context as they go.
beforeBullets: Patient selection required a multi-step modal
Procedure had to be defined upfront — no flexibility
Dropdown selection disconnected from the dental chart
afterBullets: Inline patient selection — no modal, no interruption
Procedure selection visual and tied to teeth structure

slides.label: RX Tooth Selection
slides.title: Selecting teeth should be as fast as pointing at them
slides.beforeDescription: Every tooth required its own modal — click, confirm, close, repeat. No overview, no multi-select.
slides.afterDescription: Teeth selected directly on the chart, individually or in groups, with procedures assigned inline.
beforeBullets: One modal per tooth — no multi-select
No color coding — no clinical overview
Complex cases required many repetitive steps
afterBullets: Select one tooth or many in a single interaction
Color-coded procedures visible directly on the chart
Multiple procedure groups managed in one view

slides.title: Scan & View
slides.subtitle: The active phase where clinicians capture a 3D model in real time — then step back to analyse, validate, and make decisions before anything reaches the lab.

slides.label: Scan Icons
slides.title: If users can't read the tools, they can't trust them
slides.beforeDescription: Icons had accumulated over time from different sources with no shared style or logic — each one a one-off decision, none of them part of a system.
slides.afterDescription: A unified icon set where every tool speaks the same visual language — designed to be recognised instantly, not memorised.
beforeBullets: Icons sourced from different styles with no shared visual language
No design guidelines — every new icon was an isolated decision
Similar actions looked different, breaking user expectations
Users had to learn each icon individually rather than read them intuitively
afterBullets: Unified icon set built on a single consistent style
Each icon maps clearly to its action — no guessing required
Visual consistency lets users scan the toolbar without thinking
Scalable system — new icons follow the same rules automatically

slides.label: Scan Toolbar
slides.title: Clear icons needed a consistent place to live
slides.beforeDescription: Even with better icons, tools had no permanent home. As features grew, they scattered — leaving users with no pattern to rely on mid-scan.
slides.afterDescription: A single collapsible toolbar gives every tool a fixed, predictable location — compact when focused, labelled when needed.
beforeBullets: No dedicated place for tools — each lived somewhere different
Every new feature added more scatter with no system behind it
Users had no pattern to rely on, so every scan required relearning
Searching mid-action broke physical rhythm and focus
afterBullets: All tools live in one centralised toolbar — always in the same place
Toolbar collapses to stay out of the way during active scanning
Expanding reveals tool names, so nothing requires memorisation
Scalable by design — new tools slot in without disrupting the layout

slides.label: Multi-scan & Compare
slides.title: Managing multiple scans — and comparing them
slides.content: Clinicians capture pre-treatment, post-treatment, and additional references — then need to compare them. Tabs let them switch between scans without losing context. The multi-layer panel lets them overlay those scans and adjust opacity to see differences instantly.
bullets2: Add and switch between scans within one session
Full context preserved across every tab
Overlay scans and adjust opacity in real time

slides.label: View Tools
slides.title: From icon bars to clinical decision panels
slides.beforeDescription: Tools were small icon clusters with no labels, no hierarchy, and no clinical context. Every tool looked and behaved differently — there was no pattern to learn and no clear primary action.
slides.afterDescription: Each tool became a named panel with one clear primary action and secondary options below it. Consistent structure across all tools means doctors build muscle memory fast.
beforeBullets: Small unlabelled icons — meaning had to be memorised
No visual hierarchy — all actions carried equal weight
Destructive actions sat alongside primary ones with no separation
afterBullets: Named panels with a single bold primary CTA per tool
AI detect is the default action — doctors review, not draw
Destructive actions visually separated and clearly secondary
Consistent card structure across all tools builds muscle memory

slides.label: Outcomes
slides.title: Four friction points. Four things that got better.

outcomes.title: Faster to start
outcomes.description: Removing setup gates meant clinicians could begin scanning the moment they were ready — no sequence to complete first.

outcomes.title: Scanning stayed unbroken
outcomes.description: A consistent toolbar with a clear home for every tool meant doctors stopped searching mid-scan and stayed in physical flow.

outcomes.title: Tools became readable
outcomes.description: Named panels with a single primary action replaced icon clusters — clinicians understood what to do without having to memorise anything.

outcomes.title: Results felt trustworthy
outcomes.description: Clear completion signals and visual scan comparison meant doctors stopped re-checking and started trusting what they saw the first time.
